utterances: I'm Neal Conan.
this is TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
The Russian government runs on ambiguity and plausible deniability.
That's why the story about Donald Trump Jr.'s meeting with Russians last summer may not be so clear-cut.
The lawyer he met with was described as a Russian government attorney.
The Moscow real estate developer who may have helped arrange the meeting has been described as Kremlin-linked.
NPR's Lucian Kim has more about the informal nature of the Russian government.
When Donald Trump Jr. agreed to meet Natalia Veselnitskaya last summer, she was introduced to him as a Russian government lawyer with dirt on Hillary Clinton.
But Trump Jr. says that Veselnitskaya didn't deliver the goods, and the meeting ended quickly.
In her weekly radio show last weekend, Moscow columnist Yulia Latynina said she had an explanation.
(SOUNDBITE OF ARCHIVED RECORDING)
(Speaking Russian).
Russian and American lobbyists are very different,"" Latynina said.
While Americans are trying to actually change laws, Russian lobbyists demand huge fees from their bosses by simply arranging meetings with influential people.
Mark Galeotti of the Institute of International Relations in Prague says Veselnitskaya was probably on a phishing expedition.
That's precisely the kind of thing you can then take back to Moscow and leverage.
At that point, you suddenly become interesting for the Kremlin.
So this is a classic case in my opinion of political entrepreneurship from an individual rather than some long-term planned intelligence operation.
Aras Agalarov, the Russian real estate magnate whose son may have helped organize Veselnitskaya's meeting with Trump Jr., might have been on a similar venture.
Agalarov denies he was involved.
Journalist Mikhail Zygar says despite his wealth, Agalarov has little political influence, so he may have been just providing a service for someone else.

Zygar, the author of a book called ""All The Kremlin's Men,"" says power in Russia is actually very diffuse, with government loyalists trying to anticipate what President Vladimir Putin wants without necessarily getting a direct order.
They usually do not say anything directly.
They would never say, please steal those billions of dollars, or, please murder those journalists.
They would hint.
They would say, do what you have to do.
You know your obligations.
Please fulfill them.
Those same informal relationships have been evident in Russia's use of volunteers in the war in Ukraine or patriotic hackers in the U.S.
election.
Political analyst Vladimir Frolov says that loose system of command and control creates a filter of plausible deniability for the Kremlin.
It's very convenient.
Even if, you know, things fail, there is no direct fallouts, negative fallouts for the Russian government.
The impromptu ad hoc nature of Russian governance has prompted Mark Galeotti to coin a new term, adhocracy (ph).
In the West, we're used to a system in which people's job titles actually tell you what they do.
Russia has become rather different.
It's become deinstitutionalized.
It's become what I will call an adhocracy.
Galeotti says that system may appear more nimble than ponderous Western democracies, but he warns that in the long term, it will hurt Russia.
He says it will turn the country into a pariah state and make every Russian business person or diplomat look like a potential Kremlin agent.
Lucian Kim, NPR News, Moscow.
This week, an ex-convict named Billy McCune died in an assistant living home in Kansas City.
He was 79.
In 1950, Mr. McCune, who was mentally ill, was convicted of rape and sentenced to be electrocuted.
But a song he wrote while on death row in Texas caught the ear of the governor who commuted his sentence to life in prison.
It was there almost 40 years ago that Mr. McCune met a young documentary photographer named Danny Lyon.
Danny Lyon was granted unprecedented access to photograph inside the Texas prison system.

In 1971, he published his photos in the seminal book, ""Conversations with the Dead."" Over the course of the project, Danny Lyon got to know Billy McCune, who'd become an artist in prison and ended up including many of Mr. McCune's drawings in his book.
So many, in fact, he subtitled his work ""Photographs Of Prison Life with the Letters and Drawings of Billy McCune.
Mr.
Lyon's photos became legend, but what he is not known is that he also made audio recordings of his conversations with Billy McCune.
Hidden for decades, these tapes provide a rare glimpse inside the Texas penitentiary in the 1960s and document Mr. Lyon's partnership with Billy McCune.
Mr. Lyon narrates our story.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
Is it on?
Stall now runs a small construction business.

Michigan's unemployment rate is higher than seven percent, far above the national average, and that's understandable since the manufacturing industries in the Midwest have been hit hard during the past few years.
But Robert Trumble of Virginia Commonwealth University says even people outside the rust belt are struggling to find good paying jobs.

I think there's an illusion as to how easy it'll be to get that job, and the higher you go, the more difficult it is because you're looking for something other than an entrance level job, something that pays better than an entrance level job, and those jobs are hard to come by.
And it's not just blue-collar workers.
People in public relations and corporate communications are also feeling the pinch.
Many companies are cutting costs by outsourcing their PR work.
PR consultant Melanie Rembrandt says she lost one too many jobs.
I was laid off once from a small business, and a second time from another small business, technology company, and said that's it, I'm going out on my own.
I have more job security actually running my own business than working for someone else.
She's now the CEO of Rembrandt Communications.
She says she likes being her own boss, but she's working harder than she ever did as someone else's employee.
Oh, you get to pick your hours.
I just get to pick which 12 hours a day I work.
While Rembrandt is happy with her decision to strike out on her own, not everyone in that situation feels the same.
Ken Frager has worked in public relations for more than 20 years, most recently at Johns Hopkins Hospital System in Baltimore.
He's now a freelancer.
Frager says five o'clock never comes for the self-employed, and he is responsible for absolutely everything.
I'm actually dealing with that today.
I'm having computer problems, and I moved over to my daughter's computer, so yeah, it's tough.
I don't have anybody I can call, I don't have a help desk.
Frager also complains he has trouble staying focused on his work when he's at home.
Well, I'll start working on a project; my wife will constantly tell me that there's something else that needs to be done, so before I sit down and start working, can you just sit down and do this?
That takes a lot longer than I ever expected.
Employment counselors say it's not a good thing for someone to become an independent contractor by necessity instead of choice.
For many people it's really not the move.
Not everybody is self-motivated, not everyone is disciplined, not everybody has the ability to work in an environment where they are the only employee.
Roberta Chinsky Matuson is the president of Human Resource Solutions in Massachusetts.

She says people who have been laid off and start freelancing because they can't find full-time work are most likely not prepared financially or emotionally to go it alone, but the fact remains that thousands of Americans are now self-employed because they can't find a full-time job.
To describe it, I've got a wife and a daughter who need to have regular income.
They need benefits.
I've always been the one that's provided that, and while I can get on my wife's benefits at work, they'll need some stability.
Ken Frager is still searching for that fulltime salaried position, and that overqualified construction worker?
George Stall says his business pays the bills, but sometimes only barely.
He used to get a paycheck every other week, like clockwork.
Now he gets paid by the task, not the hour, and it can 30, 60, even 90 days to see any money.
Stall says he has to watch every penny.
Rather than going out to eat, or taking the boys out to eat, rain or shine we'll barbeque in the backyard now.
Like many new independent contractors, Stall hopes to someday land a steady full-time job that comes with healthcare benefits and a retirement plan.
But Robert Trumble of Virginia Commonwealth University says for many reluctant freelancers self-employment is as good as it's going to get.
When we have a job with an employer providing these fringe benefits and so forth we have to recognize that we've got a piece of the American dream that is dwindling.
Trumble says the rise in independent contractors is serious cause for concern.
He says it most likely also means thousands more people who don't have medical insurance and aren't able to save for retirement.
Trumble warns that although companies may be saving money now, in the end, it may be taxpayers that are left holding the bill.
For NPR News, I'm Celeste Headlee in Detroit.
